chinese: 培根蛋黄意大利面 (Péigēn Dàn Huáng Yìdàlì Miàn)

region_of_origin: Rome, Italy; adapted for Singapore restaurant market
food_subgroup: Cream-based pasta dish; restaurant-prepared; ~380g per plate

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Carbonara as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent concept: pasta dish with egg and cheese sauce. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Spaghetti Carbonara as a prepared dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Spaghetti Carbonara as served at Singapore mid-range pasta restaurants (e.g., Pasta Mania, Saizeriya, Collin's), weighing approximately 380g including spaghetti, cream sauce, bacon, parmesan, and egg yolk.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti Carbonara — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti, bacon, cream, parmesan, and egg yolk, adjusted for Singapore restaurant recipe proportions
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Singapore-style Spaghetti Carbonara. Values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti (FDC 168907), bacon, heavy cream, parmesan, and egg yolk, weighted by estimated restaurant recipe proportions (~55% pasta, ~15% bacon, ~20% cream sauce, ~10% cheese/egg). Singapore HPB database consulted but no direct Carbonara entry found. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white spaghetti (al dente) as analogous food; fat from cream and bacon moderates absorption
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti cooked al dente. Singapore restaurant Carbonara uses the same spaghetti base; the addition of cooking cream and bacon fat is expected to moderately slow gastric emptying, maintaining or slightly reducing GI relative to plain spaghetti (~GI 46–58 range). GL per 380g plate calculated as: (52 × 68g carbohydrate) / 100 = 35.4. Carbohydrate per plate = 17.9 g/100g × 380g = ~68g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,049mg per 380g plate (276mg/100g × 3.8), representing 44–52% of the WHO/HPB recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000–2,400mg. Primary contributors are streaky bacon (~600mg) and parmesan cheese (~250mg).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Spaghetti Carbonara to occasional consumption; if dining out, request reduced parmesan, avoid adding salt, pair with water or plain tea, and account for this meal consuming approximately half the daily sodium budget when planning remaining meals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per plate (GL ~35) driven by approximately 68g of carbohydrate from the spaghetti portion; despite a moderate GI of ~52 (fat-slowed by cream and bacon), the absolute carbohydrate quantity produces a significant post-meal glucose excursion peaking approximately 60–90 minutes after eating.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consider consuming only half a plate (reducing GL to ~18), pairing with a zero-GL green salad instead of garlic bread, and monitoring post-meal blood glucose; the cream-based sauce does not confer a meaningful glycaemic advantage over tomato-based pasta as the carbohydrate load from the pasta is identical.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content — approximately 17.9g per 380g plate (4.7g/100g × 3.8), representing approximately 90% of the WHO recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g; combined with ~1,049mg sodium and cholesterol (~209mg per plate), this dish presents a significant cardiovascular burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Spaghetti Carbonara as an occasional indulgence only; the saturated fat from cooking cream and bacon elevates LDL cholesterol risk, and the high sodium load increases blood pressure burden — consider substituting with a tomato-based pasta (e.g., Bolognese or Arrabbiata) which delivers substantially lower saturated fat and sodium per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~17.9g/plate) and processed meat (bacon, WHO/IARC Group 1 processed meat) are associated with pro-inflammatory LPS-mediated systemic inflammation at habitual consumption.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Carbonara to occasional (monthly) consumption; weekly intake is not recommended due to the combined saturated fat and processed meat inflammatory load — Bolognese with lean mince is a lower-risk pasta alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Carbonara (Singapore Restaurant Style) is a cream-enriched pasta dish served as a main course at Singapore mid-range Western restaurants including Pasta Mania, Saizeriya, and Collin's. A standard plate weighs approximately 380g and delivers approximately 718 kcal, 24g protein, 38g fat (saturated fat ~17.9g ≈ 90% of WHO daily limit), 68g carbohydrate, and ~1,049mg sodium. The Singapore restaurant version diverges from authentic Roman Carbonara (which uses only egg yolk, guanciale, pecorino, and black pepper) by adding cooking cream to the egg yolk emulsion and substituting streaky bacon for guanciale — this increases fat content by approximately 15–20g per plate compared to the authentic preparation (~580 kcal). The sauce is rich, creamy, and mildly salty with a pronounced bacon and cheese flavour. Texture is smooth and coating, with al dente spaghetti strands. Clinically, the primary concerns are: (1) saturated fat at ~17.9g/plate driving LDL elevation and cardiovascular risk; (2) sodium at ~1,049mg/plate consuming approximately half the daily sodium budget; and (3) glycemic load of ~35 per plate creating a significant post-meal glucose excursion in diabetic patients. GI is approximately 52 (low, fat-moderated) per University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti al dente. Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Singapore restaurant-style Spaghetti Carbonara. Values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti, bacon, cream, parmesan, and egg yolk, weighted by estimated restaurant recipe proportions. Singapore HPB database has no direct Carbonara entry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (white spaghetti al dente). Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation methodology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singapore restaurant dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database; individual component fatty acid data would require a full recipe-level calculation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite prepared dish is not reported in the source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy component data for cream, bacon, and pasta — no industrial trans fats present). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero: no vitamin C-containing ingredients in standard Carbonara formulation). Portion note: input record contained an erroneous portion entry ('1 slice of cake with coffee', 300g) which has been removed as it is clearly a data entry error unrelated to this food. Two valid portions retained: 1 plate (~380g, primary serving used for GL calculation) and 1 serve ~300g. GL per serving calculated on 380g plate: (52 × 68.02g carb) / 100 = 35.4. Source: component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 168907 and related entries). GI source: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate — white spaghetti al dente, GI 52).
